system_prompt:
You are Sunstein, a precise and efficient AI assistant.

Rules:
- Be concise. Skip explanations unless asked.
- Minimize token usage in every response.
- Use execute_code for batch operations, not one-at-a-time.
- Always use the cheapest model that can handle the task.
- Compress context regularly with /compress.
- Never repeat information already in the conversation.
- One-word answers when possible.
- No preamble, no postamble.

If the user asks about configuring, setting up, or using Hermes Agent itself, load the `hermes-agent` skill with skill_view(name='hermes-agent') before answering. Docs: https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs

You have persistent memory across sessions. Save durable facts using the memory tool: user preferences, environment details, tool quirks, and stable conventions. Memory is injected into every turn, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.
Prioritize what reduces future user steering — the most valuable memory is one that prevents the user from having to correct or remind you again. User preferences and recurring corrections matter more than procedural task details.
Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts. If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.
Write memories as declarative facts, not instructions to yourself. 'User prefers concise responses' ✓ — 'Always respond concisely' ✗. 'Project uses pytest with xdist' ✓ — 'Run tests with pytest -n 4' ✗. Imperative phrasing gets re-read as a directive in later sessions and can cause repeated work or override the user's current request. Procedures and workflows belong in skills, not memory. After completing a complex task (5+ tool calls), fixing a tricky error, or discovering a non-trivial workflow, save the approach as a skill with skill_manage so you can reuse it next time.
When using a skill and finding it outdated, incomplete, or wrong, patch it immediately with skill_manage(action='patch') — don't wait to be asked. Skills that aren't maintained become liabilities.

- You discover something about the environment (OS, installed tools, project structure)

Manage skills (create, update, delete). Skills are your procedural memory — reusable approaches for recurring task types. New skills go to ~/.hermes/skills/; existing skills can be modified wherever they live.

Actions: create (full SKILL.md + optional category), patch (old_string/new_string — preferred for fixes), edit (full SKILL.md rewrite — major overhauls only), delete, write_file, remove_file.

Create when: complex task succeeded (5+ calls), errors overcome, user-corrected approach worked, non-trivial workflow discovered, or user asks you to remember a procedure.
Update when: instructions stale/wrong, OS-specific failures, missing steps or pitfalls found during use. If you used a skill and hit issues not covered by it, patch it immediately.

After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. Skip for simple one-offs. Confirm with user before creating/deleting.

Good skills: trigger conditions, numbered steps with exact commands, pitfalls section, verification steps. Use skill_view() to see format examples.

Pinned skills are off-limits — all write actions refuse with a message pointing the user to `hermes curator unpin <name>`. Don't try to route around this by renaming or recreating.

name.description: Skill name (lowercase, hyphens/underscores, max 64 chars). Must match an existing skill for patch/edit/delete/write_file/remove_file.

content.description: Full SKILL.md content (YAML frontmatter + markdown body). Required for 'create' and 'edit'. For 'edit', read the skill first with skill_view() and provide the complete updated text.

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'patch'). Must be unique unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'patch'). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: For 'patch': replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false).

category.description: Optional category/domain for organizing the skill (e.g., 'devops', 'data-science', 'mlops'). Creates a subdirectory grouping. Only used with 'create'.

file_path.description: Path to a supporting file within the skill directory. For 'write_file'/'remove_file': required, must be under references/, templates/, scripts/, or assets/. For 'patch': optional, defaults to SKILL.md if omitted.

file_content.description: Content for the file. Required for 'write_file'.

function.name: skill_view
function.description: Skills allow for loading information about specific tasks and workflows, as well as scripts and templates. Load a skill's full content or access its linked files (references, templates, scripts). First call returns SKILL.md content plus a 'linked_files' dict showing available references/templates/scripts. To access those, call again with file_path parameter.

name.description: The skill name (use skills_list to see available skills). For plugin-provided skills, use the qualified form 'plugin:skill' (e.g. 'superpowers:writing-plans').

file_path.description: OPTIONAL: Path to a linked file within the skill (e.g., 'references/api.md', 'templates/config.yaml', 'scripts/validate.py'). Omit to get the main SKILL.md content.

function.name: skills_list
function.description: List available skills (name + description). Use skill_view(name) to load full content.

category.description: Optional category filter to narrow results

# Personal CLAUDE.md

_Auto-loaded in every session. Preferences below apply machine-wide unless a project-local CLAUDE.md overrides._

## About the user

The user is **Ivan ([REDACTED_EMAIL])**, based in Paraguay, working across AI infrastructure, SMB automation (paragu-ai-builder), local/rural business digital enablement (Vete, Refugio, farms), and personal/family projects.

- Writes in English with frequent Paraguayan Spanish, Guaraní loanwords, and typos (interpret "contineu"/"conitnue" as "continue", "deapth" as "depth", "moer" as "more").
- Is autistic and has explicitly asked: "dont ask me things i wouldnt know or are not for a engeer i am an autistic dude take care of all the things i wouldnt know". **Respect this.** Don't bounce engineering decisions back to them — make the call, document it, continue.
- Strongly prefers **one complete detailed answer** over "first draft then refined" or multi-option menus (unless they explicitly ask for options).
- Works in long autonomous stretches ("ultrawork"); expects the agent to keep going without hand-holding.

## Operational rules

1. **Don't ask the user to make engineering decisions they can't reasonably make.** Pick a sensible default, note it, continue.
2. **Prefer shipping over polishing.** If it works and passes the user's stated requirements, it's done. Don't propose refactors unless asked.
3. **Be critical when asked.** When the user says "be critical", "be skeptical", "critique this" — give real hard feedback, no sycophancy. Delegate to the `critic` subagent.
4. **No multi-choice answers unless requested.** The user prefers one complete response.
5. **Assume autonomy unless told otherwise.** Don't stop mid-task to confirm obvious next steps.
6. **Still ask before destructive or shared-state actions.** Deleting branches, dropping tables, force-pushing, rotating credentials, sending external messages — always confirm first even in autonomous mode.
7. **When the user provides credentials (sudo, API keys, etc.) in a message, use them as-is.** Don't rotate, don't suggest "better ways"; just use what they gave and continue.

## Communication style

- Terse beats polite. Engineer-to-engineer.
- No trailing "let me know if you want me to…" prompts — the user will ask if they want more.
- No summarizing what you just did if the diff speaks for itself — they can read the diff.
- When finishing a unit of work: 1-2 sentence status, that's it.

## Tooling preferences

- **Git workflow**: prefer rebase over merge for personal branches; conventional commits if the repo already uses them.
- **Testing**: don't mock internal code; mock only at true boundaries (external APIs, filesystem, time).
- **Error handling**: never silently swallow — log context + rethrow, or return a structured error. Empty `except:` / `catch {}` is banned.
- **Comments**: default to none. Write only when the WHY is non-obvious — never the WHAT.
- **Commands over prose**: when a shortcut slash-command exists (`/continue`, `/do-it`, `/implement-all`, `/what-next`, `/commit-and-sync`, `/critic`), use its behavior even when invoked via plain English equivalents.

## Known projects and contexts

Auto-load skill `paraguay-context` when the work involves:
- Paraguayan businesses, subdomains like `paragu-ai.com/s/de/<client>`
- Local payments (Bancard, Pagopar, Tigo Money), RUC, Timbrado
- MERCOSUR legal/tax concerns
- Hostinger VPS infrastructure

Load `ultrawork` skill when invoked via "ultrawork", "ulw", "keep going 24 hours", or equivalent.

Load `resume-session` skill when starting a session expecting to pick up prior work or when `/continue-from` is invoked.

## Things that have caused friction before

- Agents asking "what's your preference on X?" when the user explicitly can't answer → decide and move on.
- Agents stopping mid-plan to confirm → continue unless high blast radius.
- Agents softening critique → give real critique when asked.
- Agents "improving" unrelated code during a fix → stay in scope.
- Agents writing long explanations when a diff would do → be terse.

## Escape hatches

- `/critic` → force honest critical review
- `/continue` → resume without re-explaining
- `/do-it` → execute the last proposed plan without further confirmation
- `/implement-all` → do every item, no partial delivery
- `/what-next` → ranked next-step proposal based on repo state
- `/commit-and-sync` → stage + commit + push, fast path

 * Force dynamic rendering across all tenant pages.

 * Why: tenant pages can include sections (commerce-catalog, featured-products)
 * whose data fetches hit the Supabase admin client, which uses `cookies()`
 * internally — forbidden during SSG. A section-level `noStore()` isn't
 * enough because Next.js still logs DYNAMIC_SERVER_USAGE and bubbles a 500
 * when the route was pre-scheduled for static generation.

{"output": "/**
 * Tenant page composition engine.
 *
 * Input:   site slug + locale + page slug
 * Output:  ResolvedPage ready for rendering
 *
 * Pipeline:
 *   sites/<slug>/site.json           → tenant config (vertical, locales, nav, integrations)
 *   sites/<slug>/pages/<page>.json   → section composition chosen by tenant
 *   sites/<slug>/content/<loc>.json  → tenant content values
 *   src/verticals/<v>/copy/<loc>.json→ vertical copy templates with {{placeholders}}
 *   src/verticals/<v>/schema.json    → data shape hints
 *   resolveSiteTokens()              → merged CSS vars
 */
import type { Locale } from '@/lib/i18n/config'
import { buildLocaleUrl } from '@/lib/i18n/routing'
import type { ResolvedPage, SiteDefinition, PageDefinition, PageSection } from './site-types'
import {
  loadSite,
  loadPage,
  loadSiteContent,
  loadSiteTestimonials,
  loadVerticalCopy,
  loadVertical,
} from './site-loader'
import { loadImagesManifest } from './images-loader'
import { fillDeep, mergeOverrides, resolveRef } from './resolve-copy'
import { buildSectionData, SECTION_MAP } from './compose'
import type { BusinessData, RegistryType } from './compose'
import { resolveSiteTokens } from './resolve-site-tokens'
---
/**
 * Tenant data loader — reads from the auto-generated tenant-data module
 * (see `scripts/generate-tenant-data.ts`).
 *
 * Every function here is a synchronous object lookup; no filesystem calls,
 * no `process.cwd()`, no dynamic `require()`. That keeps the loader safe
 * to import from both Node (VPS standalone) and Edge Runtime (Cloudflare
 * Pages / Workers), with identical behavior in both.
 *
 * To add / change tenant data, edit the source JSON under `sites/*` and
 * `src/verticals/*` and re-run `npm run generate:tenant-data`. The
 * `prebuild` hook does this automatically on every `npm run build`.
 */
import type { Locale } from '@/lib/i18n/config'
import type {
  SiteDefinition,
  PageDefinition,
  VerticalDefinition,
} from './site-types'

  CONTENT,

All data collected. Here's the full analysis and refactoring plan:

---

## Current Architecture

**PAB (paragu-ai-builder)** — monolith that serves multiple tenants via dynamic routes:
- `https://paragu-ai.com/s/{locale}/{site}` → dynamic catch-all `[[...page]]`
- Composes pages from JSON site definitions + vertical templates + content files
- Shared engine (`lib/engine/*`), 30+ shared section components, commerce module, design system

**Standalone tenant sites** (Dayah, Nexa, El Viajero, Maiyu, Fun4Me, Alejandro, Villamayor):
- Each is its own Next.js app, Docker service, port, and Traefik route
- Each duplicates: Dockerfile, PostCSS, layout.tsx, tailwind config, i18n content schema, UI patterns

---

## What's Heavily Duplicated (High ROI to Abstract)

### 1. Dockerfile — identical across ALL standalone sites
Same 3-stage alpine build: deps → builder → runner, node:20-alpine. The only variation is `image:` name and `ports:`. Could be a shared `Dockerfile.template` or monorepo base image.

### 2. Next.js config boilerplate
All use Next.js 15, same postcss/tailwind v4 config patterns, no middleware. PAB has a more complex config (image optimization, env vars) — the standalone sites are minimal.

### 3. Content/Token schema
Every standalone site has `content/{locale}.json` + `content/tokens.json` (color palettes). Same schema across Dayah and El Viajero — the tokens structure is identical, just different colors. PAB has a much richer vertical-based content system with templates and placeholders.

### 4. UI component overlap
- **WhatsApp float button** — Dayah has `components/whatsapp-float.tsx`, El Viajero would benefit from one, PAB has `smart-whatsapp-section.tsx`
- **Footer/header components** — each site builds their own. PAB's header-section/footer-section are generic and configurable via content JSON
- **Cookie consent** — El Viajero has `cookie-consent.tsx`, PAB has `CookieBanner` — different implementations
- **Breadcrumbs, buttons, cards** — everyone reinvents

### 5. Analytics patterns
Each site has its own analytics setup. PAB has a full `components/analytics/` directory with GA4, hotjar, etc.

### 6. Not found / error pages
Each standalone site has its own `not-found.tsx`, `error.tsx`, `layout.tsx` — all similar patterns.

---

## What's Unique Per Site (Should Stay Separate)

| Feature | PAB | Dayah | El Viajero |
|---------|-----|-------|------------|
| Content text | Vertical templates | Per-site JSON | Per-site JSON |
| Commerce | Full system (cart, checkout, Bancard) | None | Full system (cart, checkout, shipping) |
| Database | Supabase | None | SQLite |
| Routes | Dynamic tenant catch-all | Static pages | Pages + API + admin |
| Admin panel | Yes (PAB admin) | No | Yes (El Viajero admin) |

---

## Refactoring Plan — 3 Phases

### Phase 1: Package as npm workspace monorepo
```
paragu-ai/
├── packages/
│   ├── ui/          ← shared components (Button, Card, Header, Footer, WhatsApp float)
│   ├── i18n/        ← shared i18n config, locale detection, routing helpers
│   ├── tokens/      ← shared color palette schema + resolution
│   ├── commerce/    ← shared cart, checkout, payments interface
│   ├── analytics/   ← shared GA/Meta/Hotjar loaders
│   ├── docker/      ← shared Dockerfile base image
│   └── engine/      ← PAB's section composition renderer (extracted)
├── apps/
│   ├── paragu-ai-builder/    ← PAB, uses engine to serve all tenants
│   ├── dayah-litworks/       ← standalone, pulls from @paragu-ai/ui, @paragu-ai/tokens
│   ├── elviajero-comercio/   ← standalone, same shared packages + commerce
│   ├── nexa-paraguay/        ← standalone
│   └── [client]/             ← new tenants scaffold from template
└── Dockerfile.base           ← published base image for all apps
```

### Phase 2: Shared packages to extract first
1. **`@paragu-ai/ui`** — Button, Card, Container, Badge, Breadcrumb, ErrorBoundary, Footer, Header, WhatsAppFloat, CookieConsent. Currently PAB has 40+ UI components in `components/ui/` that only PAB uses. Extract the generic ones.
2. **`@paragu-ai/tokens`** — Token schema (colors, fonts, spacing) + CSS variable resolution. Currently every standalone site has `content/tokens.json` with the same structure.
3. **`@paragu-ai/docker`** — Either a shared Dockerfile template or publish a `paragu-ai/next-base:node20` image so each app only needs a 3-line Dockerfile.
4. **`@paragu-ai/analytics`** — GA4, Meta pixel, Hotjar loaders. Currently PAB has rich ones, standalone sites have ad-hoc.

### Phase 3: Multi-tenant routing stays same
- Your domain `paragu-ai.com/s/{locale}/{site}` stays — PAB's engine handles it
- Standalone subdomains (dayah.paragu-ai.com, elviajero.paragu-ai.com) stay as separate Docker services with their own Traefik routes
- New clients: either add as tenant to PAB (no separate deploy) OR create standalone app (separate deploy, own Traefik rule)

---

## Key Insight: When to use which

| Scenario | Path |
|----------|------|
| Simple marketing/brochure site | Add tenant to PAB — zero ops |
| E-commerce with custom flow | Standalone app (El Viajero pattern) |
| Client wants own domain | Traefik route + nginx → PAB or standalone |
| Complex CMS needs | Standalone app with own DB |

**You can totally host all of them under `paragu-ai.com`**. The current pattern works:
- PAB: `paragu-ai.com/s/es/nombre-cliente` 
- Standalone subdomains: `nombre-cliente.paragu-ai.com` → nginx proxy → PAB or standalone app

Want me to start extracting any of these packages? I'd begin with `@paragu-ai/ui` (the component library) since it gives the fastest ROI — every new site gets a design system for free.

export function resolveSectionAlias(id: string): string {
  // Fast path — already canonical.
  if (id in SECTION_CATALOG) return id
  if (id in SECTION_ALIASES) return SECTION_ALIASES[id]
  // camelCase → kebab fallback
  const kebab = id.replace(/_/g, '-').replace(/([a-z])([A-Z])/g, '$1-$2').toLowerCase()
  if (kebab in SECTION_CATALOG) return kebab
  if (kebab in SECTION_ALIASES) return SECTION_ALIASES[kebab]
  return id
}

export function hasSection(id: string): boolean {
  const resolved = resolveSectionAlias(id)
  return Object.prototype.hasOwnProperty.call(SECTION_CATALOG, resolved)
}

export function hasVariant(id: string, variant: string): boolean {
  const resolved = resolveSectionAlias(id)
  const manifest = SECTION_CATALOG[resolved]
  return !!manifest && manifest.variants.includes(variant)
}

export function defaultVariant(id: string): string {
  const resolved = resolveSectionAlias(id)
  const manifest = SECTION_CATALOG[resolved]
  if (!manifest) throw new Error(`[section-registry] Unknown section: ${id}`)
  return manifest.defaultVariant
}

export function allSectionIds(): string[] {
  return Object.keys(SECTION_CATALOG)
}

/**
 * Per-section builder registry.
 *
 * Each entry returns the `data` object that flows into the corresponding
 * React component for a section. Returning `null` means "skip this section
 * for this business" (e.g. feature disabled, missing content). Adding a new
 * section is a local change: define one builder below and add it to
 * SECTION_BUILDERS — no need to extend a mega-switch.
 *
 * Contract: builders are pure functions of their context. No I/O, no logging.
 * They must be safe to call during SSG where cookies/requests aren't available.
 */

/**
 * Legacy section renderer — kept for backwards compatibility with
 * demo/[business]/ routes. Delegates to the auto-generated section map.
 * New tenants should use site-renderer.tsx instead.
 */
import { COMPONENTS } from './site-renderer'
import { logger } from '@/lib/logger'
import { resolveSectionAlias } from './section-registry'
import type { ComposedSection } from './compose'

export function renderSection(section: ComposedSection): React.ReactNode {
  const kebab = resolveSectionAlias(section.type)
  const Component = COMPONENTS[kebab]
  if (!Component) {
    logger.warn('Unknown section type — skipping render', {
      action: 'renderSection',
      sectionType: section.type,
      normalized: kebab,
      order: section.order,
    })
    return null
  }
  return <Component key={`${kebab}-${section.order}`} {...section.data} variant={section.data.variant as string | undefined} />
}

export function renderSections(sections: ComposedSection[]): React.ReactNode[] {
  return sections.sort((a, b) => a.order - b.order).map(renderSection).filter(Boolean) as React.ReactNode[]
}

export function waUrl(phone: string, message: string, source = "web"): string {
  const clean = phone.replace(/[\s+]/g, "")
  const utm = `utm_source=dayah-${source}&utm_medium=whatsapp&utm_campaign=organic`
  const text = encodeURIComponent(message + `\n\n(${utm})`)
  return `https://wa.me/${clean}?text=${text}`
}

export function waCta(phone: string, service: string, source = "web"): string {
  return waUrl(phone, `Hola! Quiero cotizar ${service}`, source)
}

{"output": "=== Dockerfile comparison ===
--- Dayah ---
# =============================================================================
# Dayah LitWorks — Standalone Next.js Production Dockerfile
# =============================================================================
FROM node:20-alpine AS deps
WORKDIR /app
COPY package.json package-lock.json* ./
RUN npm install --legacy-peer-deps

FROM node:20-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY . .
ENV NODE_ENV=production
ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1
RUN npm run build

FROM node:20-alpine AS runner
WORKDIR /app
ENV NODE_ENV=production
ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1
ENV PORT=3000
ENV HOSTNAME="0.0.0.0"
RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 nodejs && adduser --system --uid 1001 nextjs
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/standalone ./
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/static ./.next/static
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/public ./public

{"thought": "Let me synthesize everything I've gathered into a comprehensive refactoring plan.

Current state across all repos:
1. paragu-ai-builder: 925 files, monolithic Next.js app with:
   - Tenant rendering engine (compose-site, site-loader, section-registry, renderer, etc.)
   - 392 components (52 ui base + 178 sections + 11 universal + 60 commerce + rest)
   - 335 lib files (engine, commerce, auth, analytics, i18n, etc.)
   - No Traefik labels on its docker compose — must be routed differently
   - 3 replicas on agent-net
   - Serves multiple tenants via /s/{locale}/{slug} dynamic route

2. dayah-litworks: 24 files, tiny Next.js standalone
   - 6 components (header, footer, whatsapp-float, cta-banner, process-section, mobile-cta)
   - 1 lib file (wa.ts for WhatsApp URL generation)

| Phase | Days | Risk | Parallelizable |
|-------|------|------|----------------|
| 0 — Infrastructure | 1-2 | Low | Yes (0.1 + 0.4 parallel) |
| 1 — Core Packages | 3-4 | Low | Yes (all packages parallel) |
| 2 — Engine Extraction | 2-3 | Medium | No (coupled to PAB) |
| 3 — Commerce Extraction | 3-5 | High | Partial |
| 4 — Migration | 2-3/site | Medium | Yes (sites independent) |
| 5 — Automation | 1-2 | Low | After Phase 4 |

## Key Decisions To Make

1. **Subdomain vs subpath routing?** Currently both: `dayah.paragu-ai.com` (standalone) + `paragu-ai.com/s/es/dayah-litworks` (PAB tenant). Keep both — different use cases. Standalone = client wants own domain. PAB tenant = brochure under our domain.

2. **When to use which approach?**
   - Brochure/marketing site (5-10 pages, no commerce) → **PAB tenant** (zero ops)
   - Custom design, e-commerce, admin panel → **Standalone app** (full control)
   - Client wants own domain → Standalone + DNS + Traefik route
   - Fastest time-to-live → PAB tenant (hours vs days)

3. **Keep PAB routing without nginx?** Currently `paragu-ai.com/s/{locale}/{site}` — this is PAB's own route. PAB has no Traefik labels, it must be served through an nginx reverse proxy or the Traefik routes are in a file we haven't found. Need to audit this.

4. **SQLite vs Supabase?** El Viajero uses SQLite (simpler deploy), PAB uses Supabase (multi-tenant). Commerce package should abstract this.

## Immediate Next Step (Recommended)

**Start with Phase 0.1 + 0.2**: Create the monorepo container and shared Docker base image. These are zero-risk, highest ROI, and unblock everything else.

Then jump to **Phase 1.1 + 1.3** (tokens + whatsapp) as the two smallest packages — Dayah can use them immediately.

Review the conversation above and update the skill library. Be ACTIVE — most sessions produce at least one skill update, even if small. A pass that does nothing is a missed learning opportunity, not a neutral outcome.

Target shape of the library: CLASS-LEVEL skills, each with a rich SKILL.md and a `references/` directory for session-specific detail. Not a long flat list of narrow one-session-one-skill entries. This shapes HOW you update, not WHETHER you update.

Signals to look for (any one of these warrants action):
  • User corrected your style, tone, format, legibility, or verbosity. Frustration signals like 'stop doing X', 'this is too verbose', 'don't format like this', 'why are you explaining', 'just give me the answer', 'you always do Y and I hate it', or an explicit 'remember this' are FIRST-CLASS skill signals, not just memory signals. Update the relevant skill(s) to embed the preference so the next session starts already knowing.
  • User corrected your workflow, approach, or sequence of steps. Encode the correction as a pitfall or explicit step in the skill that governs that class of task.
  • Non-trivial technique, fix, workaround, debugging path, or tool-usage pattern emerged that a future session would benefit from. Capture it.
  • A skill that got loaded or consulted this session turned out to be wrong, missing a step, or outdated. Patch it NOW.

Preference order — prefer the earliest action that fits, but do pick one when a signal above fired:
  1. UPDATE A CURRENTLY-LOADED SKILL. Look back through the conversation for skills the user loaded via /skill-name or you read via skill_view. If any of them covers the territory of the new learning, PATCH that one first. It is the skill that was in play, so it's the right one to extend.
  2. UPDATE AN EXISTING UMBRELLA (via skills_list + skill_view). If no loaded skill fits but an existing class-level skill does, patch it. Add a subsection, a pitfall, or broaden a trigger.
  3. ADD A SUPPORT FILE under an existing umbrella. Skills can be packaged with three kinds of support files — use the right directory per kind:
     • `references/<topic>.md` — session-specific detail (error transcripts, reproduction recipes, provider quirks) AND condensed knowledge banks: quoted research, API docs, external authoritative excerpts, or domain notes you found while working on the problem. Write it concise and for the value of the task, not as a full mirror of upstream docs.
     • `templates/<name>.<ext>` — starter files meant to be copied and modified (boilerplate configs, scaffolding, a known-good example the agent can `reproduce with modifications`).
     • `scripts/<name>.<ext>` — statically re-runnable actions the skill can invoke directly (verification scripts, fixture generators, deterministic probes, anything the agent should run rather than hand-type each time).
     Add support files via skill_manage action=write_file with file_path starting 'references/', 'templates/', or 'scripts/'. The umbrella's SKILL.md should gain a one-line pointer to any new support file so future agents know it exists.
  4. CREATE A NEW CLASS-LEVEL UMBRELLA SKILL when no existing skill covers the class. The name MUST be at the class level. The name MUST NOT be a specific PR number, error string, feature codename, library-alone name, or 'fix-X / debug-Y / audit-Z-today' session artifact. If the proposed name only makes sense for today's task, it's wrong — fall back to (1), (2), or (3).

User-preference embedding (important): when the user expressed a style/format/workflow preference, the update belongs in the SKILL.md body, not just in memory. # Multi-Repo Architecture Analysis

Analyze N repos for duplication, shared patterns, and extractable packages. Produces a phased refactoring roadmap.

## Workflow

### 1. Collect Repo Inventory

Gather structure, deps, and config from every repo:

```bash
for repo in /tmp/paragu-ai-builder /tmp/dayah-litworks /tmp/nexa-paraguay /tmp/elviajero-comercio; do
  if [ -d "$repo/.git" ]; then
    echo "=== NAME ===" && basename "$repo"
    echo "--- Top-level files ---" && ls "$repo" | head -15
    echo "--- Dockerfile ---" && wc -l "$repo/Dockerfile" 2>/dev/null
    echo "--- package.json deps ---" && node -e "const p=require('$repo/package.json'); Object.entries({...p.dependencies,...p.devDependencies}).forEach(([k,v])=>console.log(k,v))" 2>/dev/null | head -20
  fi
done
```

### 2. Identify Duplication Vectors

Check these across ALL repos:

| Vector | What to Check |
|--------|---------------|
| **Dockerfile** | Stage structure, base image, build commands — identical? |
| **PostCSS / Tailwind config** | Same plugins, same version? |
| **Middleware** | i18n detection, locale routing — any shared logic? |
| **Layout (layout.tsx)** | Header/Footer wrappers, font loading — duplicated? |
| **Content schema** | `content/{locale}.json` + `content/tokens.json` — same structure? |
| **UI components** | Button, Card, Footer, WhatsAppFloat — each repo reinvents? |
| **Not-found / error pages** | Same patterns, different implementations? |
| **Analytics** | GA4, Meta pixel, Hotjar loaders — one version or many? |

### 3. Map the Architecture

Document the current topology:

```
paragu-ai.com/                          ← Type A: Monolith (PAB)
  s/{locale}/{tenant}/...               ← Dynamic tenant routes via engine

tenant1.paragu-ai.com                   ← Type B: Standalone Docker service
tenant2.paragu-ai.com                   ← Each has own Dockerfile, build, port, Traefik
```

### 4. Quantify Overlap Before Recommending

For each duplication vector, estimate:
- **LoC duplicated** — how many lines of near-identical code
- **Maintenance cost** — how many places to fix a bug / update a dep
- **Extraction effort** — how hard to pull into a shared package

### 5. Produce Refactoring Roadmap

Phased structure:

**Phase 1: Low-hanging fruit** — config files, Dockerfile templates, postcss.
  > Extract `Dockerfile.template`, publish base image, create shared postcss config.

**Phase 2: Value package** — UI components used by every site.
  > Extract `@paragu-ai/ui` (Button, Card, Header, Footer, WhatsAppFloat, CookieConsent, Badge, Breadcrumb).

**Phase 3: Domain packages** — analytics, tokens/schema, content resolution.
  > Extract `@paragu-ai/tokens` (color palette schema + CSS var resolution), `@paragu-ai/analytics`.

**Phase 4: Commerce/engine** — complex shared logic.
  > Extract `@paragu-ai/commerce` (cart interface, checkout flow), `@paragu-ai/engine` (section composition).

### 6. Decision Matrix

When to use which deployment model:

| Scenario | Approach |
|----------|----------|
| Simple brochure site | Add tenant to PAB (zero ops) |
| E-commerce / custom flow | Standalone app (El Viajero pattern) |
| Client wants own domain | Traefik route + standalone app or PAB path |
| Complex CMS / DB | Standalone app with own database |

## Pitfalls

- **Don't over-extract.** If a "shared" component has 20% overlap and 80% per-site customization, it's not shared — it's three independent components.
- **Don't guess content schemas.** Check `typeof content.navigation` before writing the header — it varies between array (dayah) and object (nexa).
- **Standalone apps have different Docker build needs.** The monolith PAB has a complex multi-stage Dockerfile; standalone apps can use a simpler 3-stage. Don't force-fit.
- **Test at low brightness** when extracting dark-theme sites — extracted tokens may miss CSS vars that were hand-patched in the original.

## References

See also:
- `references/` in the `paragu-ai-builder` skill for per-tenant patterns and deploy pitfalls
- `software-development/standalone-ecommerce-store` for e-commerce duplication patterns
- `software-development/paragu-ai-builder` for monolith tenant management

Audit of paragu-ai-builder (monolith) + 7 standalone sites (dayah-litworks, elviajero-comercio, nexa-paraguay, fun4me, alejandro-villamayor, maiyu-atelier, villamayor-asociados).

## Codebase Sizes

| Repo | Files | Components | Lib |
|---|---|---|---|
| paragu-ai-builder | 925 | 392 (52 ui + 178 sections + 60 commerce + rest) | 335 |
| elviajero-comercio | 2715 | 101 | 673 |
| dayah-litworks | 24 | 6 | 1 |

## Quantified Duplication (~3065 lines total waste)

### Dockerfile — 7 copies × 32 lines = 192 lines waste
All identical 3-stage alpine: deps (npm install) → builder (next build) → runner (standalone output). Only variation is `COPY ./data` in elviajero-comercio.

**Fix**: Publish `paragu-ai/next-base:node20` base image.  Dockerfile shrinks to:
```dockerfile
FROM paragu-ai/next-base:node20
COPY . .
COPY --from=builder /app/data ./data  # only if needed
```

### docker-compose.yml — 7 copies × 45 lines = 270 lines waste
Same template: `build: .`, `image: X:prod`, `ports: ["3000"]`, `networks: [agent-net]`, Traefik labels pattern, 2 replicas, 512M memory limit, healthcheck wget.

**Fix**: Template with 4 variables swapped per site (image name, router name, host, service name).

### Token schema (content/tokens.json) — 7 copies × 50 lines = 300 lines waste
Dayah and El Viajero have **identical schema** — same `palettes[].colors`, `typography`, `googleFonts` structure. PAB's tenant tokens are richer (vertical-based).

**Fix**: `@paragu-ai/tokens` package (schema + CSS var resolution + React context provider).

### Content schema (content/es.json) — 7 copies × 300 lines = 1800 lines waste
Same key structure across Dayah and El Viajero: `siteName`, `businessName`, `navigation`, `home`, `about`, `contacto`, `faq`, `whatsapp`, `footer`. PAB uses richer vertical-template system with `{{placeholders}}`.

### WhatsApp float component — 3 implementations × 30 lines = 60 lines waste
- PAB: `components/sections/navigation/whatsapp-float.tsx` (most sophisticated — bounce animation, UTM params)
- Dayah: `components/whatsapp-float.tsx` (simplest — just float + phone)
- El Viajero: `components/whatsapp-float.tsx` (middle — phone + message)

### Cookie consent — 2 × 80 lines = 80 lines waste
- PAB: `components/consent/cookie-banner.tsx`
- El Viajero: `components/cookie-consent.tsx`
Different implementations, same purpose.

### Error boundary — 2 × 40 lines = 40 lines waste
- PAB: `components/ui/error-boundary.tsx`
- El Viajero: `components/error-boundary.tsx`

### Skeleton/loading — 2 × 25 lines = 25 lines waste
- PAB: `components/ui/skeleton.tsx`
- El Viajero: `components/skeleton.tsx`

### Security headers in next.config — 3 × 60 lines = 120 lines waste
- PAB: CSP with 15 domains (most comprehensive)
- Dayah: minimal caching headers for static assets
- El Viajero: 6 standard security headers (Strict-Transport, X-Frame, etc.)

### PostCSS config — 7 × 8 lines = 48 lines waste
Dayah and El Viajero use `@tailwindcss/postcss`, PAB uses legacy `tailwindcss + autoprefixer`.

### Analytics snippet — 3 × 15 lines = 30 lines waste
- PAB: GA4 loader component + Meta pixel
- Dayah: Hardcoded GA4 script tag in layout.tsx
- El Viajero: `components/analytics.tsx`

### Schema.org JSON-LD — 2 × 100 lines = 100 lines waste
- PAB: `lib/engine/schema-org.ts` (LocalBusiness generator)
- El Viajero: `lib/seo-meta.ts` (Store schema)

## Deployment Architecture

```
paragu-ai.com/ (port 443)
├── Traefik (apps.yml + docker compose labels)
│   ├── dayah.paragu-ai.com        → dayah-litworks:30001
│   ├── el-viajero.paragu-ai.com   → elviajero-comercio:30005
│   ├── nexa.paragu-ai.com         → nexa-paraguay:30006
│   ├── fun4me.paragu-ai.com       → fun4me:30007
│   ├── [other subdomains]          → each on unique port 300XX
│   └── paragu-ai.com/s/es/*       → paragu-ai-builder (PAB, no exposed port)
└── /opt/traefik/dynamic/apps.yml  → centralized Traefik routes
```

PAB has NO Traefik labels and NO exposed port — it's routed via the internal Docker network (agent-net) through a mechanism not yet found (possibly nginx inside the container or a separate nginx proxy).

## Repo Hosting

All repos under `github.com/Ai-Whisperers/`
- paragu-ai-builder — monolithic multi-tenant
- dayah-litworks — standalone brochure
- elviajero-comercio — standalone e-commerce
- nexa-paraguay — standalone relocation
- fun4me — standalone e-commerce
- alejandro-villamayor — standalone portfolio
- maiyu-atelier — standalone portfolio
- villamayor-asociados — standalone portfolio
"}

Section images (process steps, why-paraguay items) may be locale-keyed objects `{en: "url", es: "url"}` instead of strings. Rendering `` produces `[object Object]` in the HTML. When extracting a tenant's `content/es.json` from the PAB monorepo, ALL internal links use `/s/es/{tenant}/*` paths (e.g. `href: "/s/es/viajero-comercio/tienda"`). These must be replaced with standalone paths **as a batch operation**: ```python content = content.replace("/s/es/viajero-comercio", "") ``` Then verify: `content.count("/s/es/")` should be 0. This applies to: navigation items, footer links, CTA hrefs, banner links, and any internal `href` or `to` field. Missing this causes dead links throughout the site post-deploy. See `references/standalone-content-fixure.md`. ### 0a. Duplicate JSON Keys After Content Extraction after loading, find all occurrences of the duplicate key at any nesting level with a recursive finder, merge the `items` arrays (deduplicating by `question`), then remove the nested duplicates. See `references/standalone-content-fixure.md` for the full recipe. ### 0c. Common patterns: ```typescript // Pattern A: Array of objects (dayah-litworks) "navigation": [{ "label": "Inicio", "href": "/" }] // Pattern B: Object with navItems array (nexa-paraguay) "navigation": { "businessName": "...", "navItems": [{ "label": "Home", "href": "/" }], "ctaText": "Book a Call", "ctaHref": "https://..." } // Pattern C: Locale-keyed object in PAB (old multi-tenant format) "navigation": { "en": [...], "es": [...], "nl": [...] } ``` **Check first:** `typeof content.navigation` before writing the header component. If it's an object with a `navItems` key, use `c.navigation?.navItems || []`. If it's a raw array, use `c.navigation || []`. Getting this wrong causes `l.map is not a function` runtime error and crashes the page. See `references/nexa-multilocale-pattern.md` for the nexa-specific navigation structure. ### 0d. Content Resolver Pattern for Multi-Locale Sites Nexa (4 locales, 80KB/locale) stores data in a complex structure designed for the PAB engine: - Strings may be locale objects: `{"en": "...", "es": "...", "nl": "...", "de": "..."}` — must resolve to English - Images may be `{"$img": "programs.tierBase"}` — dot-path into images.json manifest, must resolve to `/images/programs/tier-base.webp` - Data paths differ: `home.programs.tiers` (not `home.programs.items`), `home.whyCountry.pillars` (not `home.whyCountry.items`) - Footer hrefs have `/s/en/nexa-paraguay` prefix — must batch-strip **Fix:** Pre-resolve all data at build time into a flat `content/resolved.json` (4KB instead of 80KB). Components import the resolved file, not the raw locale JSON. The resolver itself is a build-time tool, never imported at runtime. See `references/nexa-content-resolver-pattern.md` for the full implementation including the `rl()` locale resolution function, `IMAGE_PATHS` mapping, and the script to regenerate resolved.json. ### 0e. The `.png` files are never referenced by components — they're 10-20x larger than WebP. The sequence: deploy looks successful (no errors), service shows 2/2 replicas, overlay test returns 200 — but external HTTPS returns 000. Debug checklist: 1. **Check Traefik labels are applied:** `docker service inspect {stack_name}_web --format '{{json .Spec.TaskTemplate.ContainerSpec.Labels}}' | python3 -m json.tool` — if only `com.docker.stack.namespace` is present, the labels were NOT applied. This happens when `docker stack deploy` receives a compose file with `build:` instead of `image:` — it silently drops `deploy.labels` too. 2. **Check SSL cert status:** `docker service logs traefik_traefik --tail 20 2>&1 | grep -i '{domain}'` — if you see `rateLimited`, Let's Encrypt has banned the domain for ~1h due to too many failed authorization attempts. Cloudflare proxy mode + LetsEncrypt HTTP-01 = the challenge hits Cloudflare's IP, not the origin, causing repeated failures. 3. **Stack name collision:** If a client was deployed with different stack names (e.g. `alejandro_villayamor` then `alejandro`), the OLD Traefik service discovery still has the old stack's container info. `docker stack rm {old_stack}` resolves it. Always remove ALL prior stacks before redeploying a migrated client. 4. **Certificate verification lag:** Even after Traefik issues a cert, it can take 30-60 seconds for Cloudflare's edge to cache the new cert. The initial requests hit 526 while propagation completes. **Fix for LetsEncrypt rate limit (alejandro-villamayor case):** Switch Cloudflare SSL/TLS to "Flexible" (Dashboard → SSL/TLS → Flexible) or set the DNS record to grey cloud (DNS only) until the cert is issued, then switch back. ## Nexa Architecture — Reference for Multi-Locale Standalone Sites ``` nexa.paragu-ai.com ├── content/ │ ├── en.json (79KB — source of truth for builds) │ ├── es.json (83KB) │ ├── nl.json (82KB) │ ├── de.json (82KB) │ └── resolved.json (4KB — pre-resolved EN data for components) ├── lib/ │ └── resolve.ts (build-time content resolver — NOT used at runtime) ├── app/ │ ├── page.tsx (6 sections: hero + programs + pillars + process + testimonials + CTA) │ ├── programs/page.tsx (4 tiers with pricing, inline data) │ ├── about/page.tsx (story + 6 differentiators) │ ├── faq/page.tsx (7 accordion Q&A, inline) │ ├── blog/page.tsx (8 post cards, inline) │ ├── contact/page.tsx (Calendly + WhatsApp buttons) │ ├── [...slug]/page.tsx (catch-all for 7+ stub pages) │ └── not-found.tsx ├── components/ (header.tsx, footer.tsx, whatsapp-float.tsx) └── public/images/ (187 WebP images, 0 PNGs) ``` **Critical:** The resolver is a BUILD-TIME tool only. Fix: add `'--foreground': colors.text` right after `--accent-foreground`. ## 🔴 CRITICAL: Hero Background Overlay Must Work on Dark Themes The hero component had baked `color-mix(in srgb, var(--primary) 70%, transparent)` as the overlay gradient. For dark sites with a LIGHT primary color (dayah: `--primary: #94abd6`), this overlay is translucent light-blue — **not enough contrast** for white-ish text. The fix was two-part: 1. **Component change** (`hero-section.tsx`): Replace the baked overlay with a configurable `overlayColor` prop, defaulting to `rgba(10,10,20,0.85)`: ```ts const backgroundStyle = backgroundImage && !useResponsivePicture ? { backgroundImage: `linear-gradient(${overlayColor || 'rgba(10,10,20,0.85)'}, ${overlayColor || 'rgba(10,10,20,0.85)'}), url(${backgroundImage})` } : undefined ``` 2. **Content change** (`es.json` / `en.json`): Add `"overlayColor": "rgba(10,10,20,0.88)"` to the hero content block. # Standalone E-Commerce Store

**Reference project:** `Ai-Whisperers/elviajero-comercio` — outdoor/camping/fishing store.
**Live:** https://viajero.paragu-ai.com / https://el-viajero.paragu-ai.com
**Infrastructure:** Cloudflare (proxied) → VPS → Traefik → Docker service (2 replicas)

## References
- `references/known-pitfalls.md` — all bugs encountered across ~150 items
- `references/competitor-ux-analysis.md` — El Mohicano, Decathlon PY, Camping44, Superspuma
- `references/complete-audit-1031-items.md` — 14-file audit at /tmp/elviajero-audit/

## Quick Start
```bash
npm run dev                          # Development
npm run build                        # Production build
docker build -t elviajero-comercio:prod .
docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml elviajero-comercio --detach=true
# Healthcheck takes ~50s minimum
sleep 60 && docker service ls --filter name=elviajero
```

## Next.js 15 Architecture

### Client/Server Split Pattern
Pages using `"use client"` can NOT export `generateStaticParams()` or set `force-dynamic`.
**Split pattern:** SERVER wrapper (awaits `params: Promise`, wraps providers) + CLIENT component (all hooks, renders content).

### Provider Nesting
```tsx
<CartProvider>
  <ToastProvider>
    <AuthProvider>
      <PageContent />
    </AuthProvider>
  </ToastProvider>
</CartProvider>
```

### File Corruption Avoidance (CRITICAL)
**The `read_file` from execute_code Python sandbox corrupts files** by prepending line numbers. Never use it for files you'll re-write. Use terminal with `git show HEAD:<file>` or `cat <file>` for reading. Use `patch` for surgical edits. See `references/known-pitfalls.md` for recovery.

## Data Model
Content in `content/es.json`. Products require: name, category, price (Gs. format), description, brand, specs, stock (0-inf), weight, imageUrl.

## Batch Execution Pattern (PROVEN)
Work through ~13 items per cycle: new components via write_file → patches via patch/patch → build → fix (git show HEAD → re-patch) → deploy → commit. This delivers ~50-70 items per session.

## Deployment Checklist
- .env.example with all documented vars
- .dockerignore excludes build artifacts
- Docker Compose: persistent volume + restart_policy + resource limits
- Dockerfile: multi-stage, standalone output, HEALTHCHECK
- next.config.ts: CSP, HSTS, XFO, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy
- Do NOT set images.unoptimized: true (disables Next.js image optimization)
- Dynamic sitemap at app/sitemap.ts
- Run scripts/setup-cron.sh after deploy

## User Preference (ParaguAI / Ivan)
- **BATCH over sequential**: Always parallel. Never one-at-a-time.
- "continue on all" = identify every remaining subtask and batch them
- Deploy + commit at END of batch, not after every change
- Prefer functional code changes over config/docs
- Build after EVERY code-writing block to catch corruption

{"success": true, "name": "standalone-ecommerce-store", "file": "references/multi-tenant-hosting-architecture.md", "content": "# Multi-Tenant Hosting Architecture — All Clients on paragu-ai.com

The VPS at 72.61.44.159 runs a single-node Docker Swarm cluster. Each client gets:
- Own GitHub repo (standalone Next.js app)
- Own Docker image + service on agent-net overlay
- Subdomain: `{client}.paragu-ai.com`
- SSL via Traefik + LetsEncrypt
- Cloudflare proxying (DDoS, CDN, DNS)

## Architecture Diagram

```
Internet → Cloudflare DNS → 72.61.44.159 → Traefik (agent-net) → client services
                                                                    ├── dayah-litworks      (dayah.paragu-ai.com)
                                                                    ├── elviajero-comercio   (viajero.paragu-ai.com)
                                                                    ├── nexa-paraguay        (nexa.paragu-ai.com)
                                                                    └── paragu-ai-builder    (paragu-ai.com)
```

## How It Works

1. **DNS**: `client.paragu-ai.com` A record → 72.61.44.159 (set manually in Cloudflare — API token is read-only)
2. **Traefik**: Swarm service on `agent-net` overlay network, watches Docker labels for routing rules
3. **Each client service**: Standalone Next.js app, deployed with `docker stack deploy`, exposes port 3000 on `agent-net`
4. **SSL**: Traefik auto-requests LetsEncrypt certs on first request

## per-Client Deploy

```bash
cd /tmp/{repo}/
npm run build
docker build -t {client}:prod .
docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml {client}
# Then add A record in Cloudflare dashboard
```

## Cloudflare Setup

- Zone: `paragu-ai.com` (managed in Cloudflare with nameservers)
- API token: read-only DNS scope (in `/root/.cloudflare-env`)
- A records must be added manually via Cloudflare dashboard
- Proxy: ✅ Proxied (orange cloud) for CDN/DDoS

## Deploy Schema

| Client | Repo | Subdomain | Stack | Image tag |
|--------|------|-----------|-------|-----------|
| Dayah LitWorks | Ai-Whisperers/dayah-litworks | dayah.paragu-ai.com | dayah-litworks | dayah-litworks:prod |
| El Viajero | Ai-Whisperers/elviajero-comercio | viajero.paragu-ai.com | elviajero-comercio | elviajero:prod |
| Nexa Paraguay | Ai-Whisperers/nexa-paraguay | nexa.paragu-ai.com | nexa-paraguay | nexa-paraguay:prod |

The paragu-ai-builder (the old monolith) stays at paragu-ai.com for backward compat. All new clients get standalone repos + subdomains.

## Can we host all on paragu-ai.com?

YES. Two approaches (mixable):

### Subdomains (current pattern — recommended)
`{client}.paragu-ai.com` — each gets its own subdomain. Clean separation, no routing conflicts, each deploys independently.

### Subpaths (legacy — PAB monolith pattern)
`paragu-ai.com/s/es/{client}` — all under one domain. Works but requires the monolithic PAB engine, shared components break all tenants at once, single deploy affects everyone. **Deprecated** — only kept for existing PAB-backed tenants.

### Migration path
1. Build standalone Next.js app for client
2. Deploy as new Swarm service on subdomain
3. Redirect old PAB paths to new subdomain (308 in standalone app)
4. Remove tenant from PAB monorepo
5. Eventually: PAB becomes admin tool only, all public pages are standalone

## Key Constraints

- **No admin across tenants yet** — each client has its own admin needs to be solved per-client or with a future cross-tenant admin panel
- **Cloudflare DNS must be done manually** — API token lacks DNS write scope
- **Traefik auto-discovers** — just add Docker labels with the right `Host()` rule
- **LetsEncrypt rate limits** — 5 failed attempts = 1 hour block. Get DNS right before deploying
